problem_summary: 2021 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability): build a model of a food system that measures the trade-off between maximizing efficiency/output and optimizing for equity and sustainability, apply it to two contrasting nations, and discuss how to adjust priorities and scale the model to smaller/larger regions. This O-award paper (Team 2119893) frames the whole problem as a weighted multi-indicator evaluation index optimized by two extra "superior" factors (Equity and Sustainability), then layers a cost-benefit / utility-maximization analysis on top to find a "revenue equilibrium point" distinguishing developing (Kenya) from developed (Greece) countries.
models_used: Hierarchical multi-indicator evaluation index: 5 superior indicators (Food, Society, Economy, Efficiency, Emergency) decomposed into 24 inferior indicators, aggregated as linear combination V = sum(omega_i * I)
Combination weighting = AHP (subjective) x Entropy Weight Method (objective), combined via geometric mean W_j = sqrt(alpha_j*beta_j)/sum(sqrt(alpha_j*beta_j))
Entropy Weight Method (EWM): information entropy E_j over normalized p_ij to get objective weights

Four-method normalization toolkit: (1) graded membership function (piecewise rational + c*ln x + d) for fuzzy indicators, (2) Sigmoid for unbounded indicators like population, (3) max/min/optimal-value linear normalization for benefit/cost/optimum-type indicators, (4) Z-score for approximately-normal indicators
Emergency sub-model: Poisson distribution for disaster frequency N~P(mu), exponential distribution for event significance and per-indicator impact delta
Climate/carbon sub-model: per-capita CO2-eq accounting for plant products, animal products (C x EF), and transport (C x EF x D), borrowed from Vicente 2021

Planetary Boundaries framework for Nitrogen/Phosphorus flows, Freshwater Use (environmental flow requirements EFR), Biodiversity loss (E/MSY extinction rate), and Land-system change (cropland-use 15% boundary)
Land-system value model: v(a) = sum(c_i*A_0) + logistic-growth impact term l(a), where l solves Verhulst/logistic ODE dl/da = C_0*l(1 - l/k) giving l(a)=K/(K*e^(-C_0*a)+1)
Optimization overlay: V_C = v_i * i + epsilon_i mapping Equity (Race, Food Safety, Gender, Policy) and Sustainability (5 planetary-boundary factors) onto the basic indicators

Cost-benefit / microeconomic priority model: developing-country profit maximization & cost minimization (pi_max = sum p_i*y_i - min sum w_i*x_i s.t. production function); developed-country utility maximization max U(x)

Equilibrium-point analysis: set developing total revenue R^0 = alpha_i(i - v_i*i_0 - epsilon_i) equal to developed revenue R = sigma*GDP, solve R^0 = R for the threshold sigma where a developing country 'becomes' developed
sigma composite economic-welfare factor built from GINI coefficient (equity), Environmental Kuznets Curve (sustainability), and Income Elasticity of demand (society)
Sensitivity analysis: one-at-a-time perturbation (0.1%-5%) of carbon emission, biodiversity, women's rights, GDP
Scalability: 'splitting' larger regions (e.g. Asia: 4 developed + 45 developing) into sub-groups, averaging V, and re-deriving a new equilibrium point; re-weighting indicators for smaller regions
key_techniques: Two-layer architecture: a defensible 'basic' evaluation index first, then Equity & Sustainability bolted on as an optimization overlay (V_C = v_i*i + epsilon_i) so the marginal effect of the contest's core theme is isolated and measurable

Demonstrating that optimizing for equity/sustainability HELPS the developed country (Greece 0.423 -> 0.445, +5.2%) but HURTS the developing one (Kenya 0.350 -> 0.294, -16.0%) - a counterintuitive, narrative-driving result that directly answers the prompt's trade-off question

Bridging an engineering evaluation index to economics: reinterpreting the index as 'total revenue' and using cost-benefit + utility maximization to give developing vs developed countries genuinely different objective functions

Defining a single dynamic control parameter sigma whose equilibrium value marks the developing->developed transition, turning a qualitative 'priorities differ' point into a numeric threshold (Greece sigma=0.44494, Kenya 0.27036, Asia 0.34432)

Heavy, citation-anchored borrowing of recognized frameworks (Planetary Boundaries, EAT-Lancet/Willett, Environmental Kuznets Curve, GINI, Verhulst logistic) to lend authority to otherwise hand-built sub-models
Method-selection-as-rigor: explicitly comparing four normalization methods and two weighting philosophies, then justifying the combined choice, which reads as methodological care to judges
Using probability distributions (Poisson + exponential) to model the otherwise-unquantifiable 'Emergency' dimension as a stochastic shock term epsilon
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness and narrative, not on any single deep model. It answers every part of the prompt (build model, test on two countries, handle priorities, scale up/down) with a clean, modular pipeline. Its standout move is producing a concrete, counterintuitive headline result - equity/sustainability optimization raises the developed country's score but lowers the developing country's - which is exactly the efficiency-vs-equity tension the problem asks about, and it backs that with numbers, radar charts, and a sensitivity analysis showing Greece is sensitive to carbon/biodiversity/gender while Kenya is sensitive to GDP. The economics overlay (cost-benefit + utility + a sigma equilibrium point) is genuinely creative differentiation that most teams would not attempt, giving developing and developed countries different objective functions rather than one score. Combined with disciplined method selection (four normalization methods compared, AHP x entropy combination weighting) and a scalability section that actually re-derives an equilibrium for Asia, the paper signals breadth, self-awareness, and a coherent story - the qualities O-award judges reward."
section_highlights: "Summary: dense, sequential, and quantitative - it states the actual scores (0.445/0.294 with vs 0.423/0.350 without) and the equilibrium parameters in the abstract, so a judge knows the findings before reading. Section 3 (model build) is the backbone: clear superior/inferior decomposition, a normalization sub-section that compares methods, and a weighting sub-section that motivates the AHP+entropy combination. Section 4 (Greece vs Kenya) pairs a results table with two radar charts and a one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis - concrete, visual, and interpreted ('the system sacrifices economy and efficiency to gain equity'). Section 5 (priorities) is the intellectual high point, recasting indicators as Basic Needs vs Higher Pursuit and introducing the cost-benefit/utility/equilibrium machinery. Section 6 (scalability) handles the smaller-region (re-weight) and larger-region ('splitting' Asia) cases distinctly. Strengths/Weaknesses is honest (admits area-change effects are neglected and that war/severe emergencies break the model). The Irish-proverb epigraph and consistent figure captions give it a polished, deliberate feel."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many formulas are asserted, not derived or validated - e.g. the food coefficient v_fd = 1 - cuberoot(lambda_pr*lambda_te*lambda_ar) and the efficiency formula v_ef = alpha*v_ec*PD^(...) are dimensionally and physically hand-waved with no calibration
Parameter values are largely unsourced or arbitrary: emergency 'general significance' fixed at 0.001, exponential rates of 1000, membership-function anchor points f(3)=0.8, f(1)=0.01 chosen by fiat
The Z-score normalization (eq 14) does not map to [0,1] as the section claims, contradicting the stated goal of bounding indicators in [0,1]; method definitions are loose

The central optimization equation V_C = v_i*i + epsilon_i and revenue equation R^0 = alpha_i(i - v_i*i_0 - epsilon_i) are under-specified - it is never fully shown how the 9 equity/sustainability sub-models feed concrete numbers into v_i, so reproducibility is weak
Only two countries tested, with no out-of-sample or robustness check beyond a narrow one-at-a-time sensitivity sweep (0.1%-5%); OAT ignores interactions

Internal inconsistencies/sloppiness: radar-chart numbers for Greece (Society/Efficiency 0.771/0.854) do not match Table 3, equilibrium sigma for Kenya is quoted as both 0.27036 and 0.26847, several figures/mermaid diagrams are garbled ('House Icon' chains, Egypt vs Kenya slip in 1.2)

The cost-benefit/equilibrium layer is conceptually appealing but mathematically thin - R = sigma*GDP with sigma 'integrating' GINI + Kuznets + income elasticity is never given an explicit functional form
Data provenance is vague ('we collected data', World Bank/FAO/IFA mentioned) with no data table or units for the actual country inputs
reusable_patterns: The two-layer evaluation template: build a defensible baseline weighted index first, then add the contest's signature theme as a separate optimization overlay so you can quantify its marginal effect (delta score) - directly portable to any 'measure and optimize a system' prompt
Combination weighting (AHP x EWM via geometric mean) as a default robust weighting scheme that pre-empts the 'your weights are subjective' critique

A reusable normalization toolkit: membership-function for fuzzy/graded, Sigmoid for unbounded, max/min/optimum-linear for benefit/cost/optimum types, Z-score for normal - and the rhetorical move of comparing methods before choosing
Model rare shocks as a stochastic term: Poisson(frequency) x Exponential(magnitude) to add an 'Emergency/resilience' dimension to any index without needing real disaster data
Borrow a named planetary/economic framework per sub-dimension (Planetary Boundaries, Kuznets, GINI, logistic growth) to instantly add credibility and citations to hand-built components

The equilibrium-point trick: define one tunable priority parameter, write two different objective functions for two regimes (here developing profit-max vs developed utility-max), set them equal, and solve for the threshold - a clean way to produce a single defensible number that distinguishes scenarios

Scalability via 'splitting': decompose a large heterogeneous region into homogeneous sub-groups, compute per-group scores, then aggregate/average and re-derive the headline metric - and re-weight (not rebuild) indicators for smaller regions
Front-load quantitative results into the summary/abstract and pair every country comparison with a radar chart + interpreted sentence
Write an honest Weaknesses section that names the regime where the model breaks (here: war/severe emergencies, area-dependent indicator shifts) - judges reward self-awareness
